title: Create teachingContent schema and style guide for all system design templates
description: The teaching quality audit found ALL 55 templates score F (0-11/36) on teaching dimensions because the content format only supports architecture description + technical steps. No hooks, no intuition, no edge cases, no comparisons, no common mistakes, no self-tests, no summaries exist ANYWHERE. Root cause: the JSON schema doesn't require educational content. Fix: Add a 'teachingContent' section to the template schema with required fields: hook (problem-first 2-3 sentences), analogy (real-world metaphor), whyThisApproach (per step), edgeCases (array), commonMistakes (array), comparison (vs alternatives), summary (3 bullet takeaway). Write a style guide document explaining how to write each field with examples. This is the SINGLE HIGHEST IMPACT task — it enables all subsequent content improvement.
acceptanceCriteria: Template JSON schema extended with teachingContent section (7 required sub-fields)
Style guide document created at docs/guides/template-content-guide.md
Style guide includes 3 fully-written examples (URL Shortener, Chat System, Rate Limiter)
UI components updated to render new teachingContent fields in the canvas/panels
Existing templates load without error (teachingContent is optional for backwards compatibility)

title: Rewrite URL Shortener teachingContent — flagship template for #1 interview question
description: URL Shortener is the most common system design warm-up question and our entry point for beginners. Current score: 11/36 (F). Missing: hook, intuition, complexity intuition, edge cases, comparison, mistakes, summary. REWRITE with: Hook: 'bit.ly handles 500M clicks/day — how would you build one?' Analogy: 'Like a library card catalog — each card (short URL) points to a book (long URL), and the catalog must handle millions of lookups per second.' Per-step WHY: explain why KGS over hashing (collision risk), why cache (100:1 read ratio), why 301 vs 302 (speed vs analytics). Edge cases: cache crash cascade, key exhaustion. Mistakes: 'truncating MD5 causes collisions after ~1B URLs (birthday problem).' Summary: 3-bullet flashcard takeaway.
acceptanceCriteria: Hook opens with real-world scale and hooks curiosity
Analogy provides intuitive mental model before architecture
Each learnStep has 1-2 sentences explaining WHY this approach over alternatives
2 edge cases documented with simulation cross-references
2 common mistakes listed with explanations
3-bullet memorable summary suitable for flashcard export

title: Rewrite Chat System (WhatsApp) teachingContent with WebSocket motivation
description: Chat System is a top-5 interview question. Current score: 11/36. Missing: hook, intuition about real-time, why WebSocket over polling, edge cases for offline delivery. REWRITE with: Hook: 'WhatsApp delivers 100 billion messages per day — how do you ensure every message arrives, even when the recipient is offline?' Analogy: 'Like a post office that knows whether you are home (online) or away (offline), and holds your mail until you return.' WHY WebSocket: 'Polling wastes bandwidth checking for nothing; WebSocket is a permanent phone line.' Edge: offline queue overflow, out-of-order delivery. Mistake: 'Students forget about message ordering per conversation — without Kafka partitioning by conversationId, messages arrive scrambled.'
acceptanceCriteria: Hook establishes scale and the offline delivery problem
WebSocket vs polling comparison with clear winner explanation
Offline delivery edge case explained with queue overflow scenario
Message ordering pitfall highlighted as common mistake

title: Rewrite Rate Limiter with progressive algorithm comparison narrative
description: Rate Limiter is a top-10 interview question. Current score: 8/36. The biggest teaching gap: steps explain each algorithm in isolation but never show WHY algorithm A fails, leading to algorithm B. REWRITE with progressive narrative: (1) Fixed Window — simple but the boundary burst problem (show that 2N requests can pass at window edge). (2) Sliding Window — fixes boundary but memory-intensive. (3) Token Bucket — handles bursts naturally. Each step opens with the FLAW of the previous approach. Hook: 'Without rate limiting, one misbehaving client can take down your entire service. Twitter once had a user send 100K API calls per minute.' Mistake: 'Students implement token bucket but forget that distributed systems need shared state — local counters allow N × limit across N servers.'
acceptanceCriteria: Progressive narrative: each algorithm introduced because the previous one had a flaw
Fixed window boundary burst problem explicitly visualized/explained
Token bucket burst capacity explained as a feature, not just a mechanism
Distributed rate limiting challenge (shared state) as a key teaching moment
Common mistake about local vs shared counters highlighted

title: Rewrite Twitter/X Timeline with fanout strategy motivation
description: Twitter Timeline is a top-10 interview question about the push vs pull tradeoff. Current score: 11/36. Missing: hook, intuition about fanout, why hybrid approach. REWRITE with: Hook: 'When Taylor Swift tweets to her 90M followers, Twitter must update 90 million timelines instantly. But when you tweet to your 200 followers, the same approach would be 450,000x wasteful. How does Twitter handle both?' WHY fanout-on-write for normal users: 'Pre-computed feeds = fast reads.' WHY fanout-on-read for celebrities: 'Writing to 90M feeds takes 90 seconds — the tweet is stale before some followers see it.' Edge: viral retweet cascade, deleted tweet tombstones. Mistake: 'Students apply one strategy to all users — the hybrid approach is the key insight.'
acceptanceCriteria: Hook quantifies the celebrity vs normal user scaling problem
Hybrid fanout strategy explained as the KEY interview insight
Celebrity threshold logic explained (1M+ followers = pull model)
Deleted tweet handling mentioned as edge case
Summary explicitly states: 'The hybrid approach IS the answer interviewers want'

title: Rewrite Netflix CDN with ABR streaming intuition
description: Netflix CDN is a top-10 interview question. Current score: 11/36. Missing: hook about streaming scale, intuition about why CDNs exist (latency physics), ABR as user-experience innovation. REWRITE with: Hook: 'Netflix accounts for 15% of all global internet traffic. During peak hours, 250 million subscribers stream simultaneously. One bottleneck anywhere means millions of buffering wheels.' Analogy: 'CDN edge servers are like neighborhood libraries — instead of everyone driving to the central library (origin), your neighborhood has a copy of the popular books (content).' WHY ABR: 'Without ABR, your video would either buffer constantly on slow networks or look terrible on fast ones. ABR adapts in real-time, switching quality mid-stream without the user noticing.' Edge: CDN miss during new content launch. Mistake: 'Students put ALL content on CDN — in reality only the top 20% of content is pre-positioned, because storage at edge is expensive.'
acceptanceCriteria: Hook establishes Netflix's internet-scale traffic
CDN analogy (neighborhood library) builds intuition before architecture
ABR explained as a user-experience innovation, not just a protocol
Two-path architecture (API vs streaming) motivated as a scaling insight
Mistake about CDN storage economics highlighted

title: Add teachingContent to 10 next-priority templates (batch rewrite)
description: After the top-5 flagship templates are rewritten, apply the teachingContent style guide to the next 10 most important templates: (1) Distributed Cache, (2) Uber/Ride-Sharing, (3) Food Delivery, (4) Collaborative Editor, (5) Event Sourcing, (6) Social Feed, (7) Web Crawler, (8) Hotel Reservation, (9) Email Service, (10) Stock Exchange. Each template needs: hook, analogy, per-step WHY, edge cases, common mistakes, comparison with alternatives, 3-bullet summary.
acceptanceCriteria: 10 templates have complete teachingContent sections
Each template has: hook, analogy, 2+ edge cases, 2+ mistakes, summary
Content is specific to each template (not generic boilerplate)
All content verified against authoritative sources (DDIA, Alex Xu, engineering blogs)

title: Add teachingContent to remaining 35 templates (systematic batch)
description: After top-5 and next-10 are rewritten, apply teachingContent to all remaining 35 templates. This can be done in batches of 7-10 per sprint. Each template needs the same structure: hook, analogy, per-step WHY, edge cases, mistakes, comparison, summary. Use the style guide and existing rewrites as reference. Lower-priority templates (infrastructure, difficulty 3) can have shorter content but must meet the minimum bar.
acceptanceCriteria: All 55 templates have teachingContent sections (no F-grade templates remain)
Minimum: hook + 1 edge case + 1 mistake + summary per template
Content quality consistent with the style guide examples

title: Add 'Common Mistakes' section to template UI rendering
description: Teaching audit found T8 (Common Mistakes) scores 0.0/3 across ALL 55 templates — completely absent. This is the highest-impact teaching gap. Create a UI component that renders 'Common Mistakes' from the teachingContent.commonMistakes array. Display in the LearnSteps panel or Properties panel as a collapsible 'Watch Out' section with warning-icon styling. Should be visually distinct (amber/yellow) to draw attention.
acceptanceCriteria: CommonMistakes component renders array of mistake strings from template JSON
Styled with warning icon and amber background to draw attention
Collapsible by default (expanded on first view, collapsed on revisit)
Visible in the learning flow (not buried in settings)

title: Add 'Memorable Summary' / flashcard takeaway to template UI
description: Teaching audit found T12 (Memorable Summary) scores 0.0/3 across ALL templates. No concise takeaway exists anywhere. Create a UI component that renders a 3-bullet summary from teachingContent.summary. Display at the bottom of the LearnSteps progression as the final 'card'. Should be styled like a flashcard — distinct visual treatment (bordered card with key icon). Include a 'Copy to clipboard' button for study notes.
acceptanceCriteria: Summary component renders 3 bullet points from template JSON
Styled as a distinct 'flashcard' card with copy-to-clipboard button
Appears as the final step in the LearnSteps progression
Content is concise enough to memorize (each bullet < 20 words)

title: Add 'Hook' section to template header in canvas UI
description: Teaching audit found T1 (Hook) scores 1.0/3 — every template opens with a dry definition instead of a motivating question. Create a UI component that renders the teachingContent.hook as the FIRST thing a user sees when loading a template. Display above the learnSteps, styled as an attention-grabbing intro (larger font, question mark icon, maybe a brief stat or scale number). This replaces the current generic description as the first impression.
acceptanceCriteria: Hook displayed prominently when template loads (above learnSteps)
Styled to grab attention (larger font, distinct background, question/scale stat)
Falls back gracefully to description if hook is missing
Visible on both desktop and mobile layouts

title: Add 'Predict Before Reveal' self-test prompts to learnStep transitions
description: Teaching audit found T11 (Self-Test) scores 0.0/3 across ALL templates. No prediction or testing opportunity exists anywhere. Add a 'Predict' prompt between learnSteps: before revealing step N+1, show a question like 'What do you think happens when the cache fails?' or 'Which component should handle offline messages?' User clicks to reveal the answer (next step). This transforms passive reading into active learning. Research shows prediction-before-reveal improves retention 2-3x (Brilliant.org methodology).
acceptanceCriteria: Prediction prompts appear between learnSteps (before step content reveals)
Each prompt is a specific question about what the NEXT step will cover
User clicks 'Reveal Answer' to see the next learnStep
Prompts are populated from teachingContent.selfTestQuestions array
Can be skipped (not forced) for users who want to browse quickly
